terms.definition: The systemic property denoting the alignment and mutual reinforcement of a system’s constituent parts, enabling collective action at minimal internal friction.
A scalar index between 0 and 1 operationalising the measurable coherence of a civilisation; higher values denote greater systemic alignment.
A communication environment in which most information is implicit and derived from shared backgrounds rather than explicit statements.
The social practice of marrying exclusively within a specified in-group.
A theological–cultural ideal in Amish life implying yieldedness or submission of individual will to communal authority.
A governance structure in which decision-making power is dispersed across multiple nodes, preventing unilateral control.
The direct passage of knowledge, values, and practices from one age cohort to the next within a community.
Social interaction occurring within a group size that allows all members to recognise and recall each other personally, typically ≤150 individuals.
An observational study leveraging naturally occurring contrast groups that share macro-conditions but differ on target variables; lacks random assignment.
